metric_name: Pro-E Bench97: AlphaStation 500au vs SGI best Unix workstation
metric_value: Under 60 min vs 80+ min (Digital wins)
notes: Entry-level Digital 500au ran Pro-E Bench97 in under 60 minutes; best Unix-based SGI workstation took over 80 minutes.
Personal Workstation 600au (600 MHz Alpha) started at $10,000.

metric_value: 12 CPUs
notes: AlphaServer 8400 supported up to 12 CPUs; 8200 up to 6.

metric_value: Crossbar switch Memory Channel
notes: TruCluster used Memory Channel crossbar switch for dynamic connections of multiple SMP nodes.

metric_value: Very Large Memory (VLM) support beyond 2GB
notes: Digital Unix 64-bit with VLM capabilities enabled use of more than 2GB main memory.

metric_value: 132 and 264 MB/sec PCI I/O
notes: AlphaServers incorporated fast 132 and 264 MB/sec industry-standard PCI I/O.

metric_value: Lack of NT transition commitment differentiates from Digital
notes: Aberdeen noted Digital's NT support differentiated it from SGI; cited Pro-E benchmark as evidence.

metric_value: 32-bit MPP; overly expensive for typical HPTC; slow-to-market in 64-bit Unix
notes: Aberdeen characterized IBM's 32-bit MPP RS/6000 SP as expensive and slow; favorable in chess (Deep Blue) but not typical production HPTC.

metric_value: Not investing in Convex HPTC; too small a portion of HP
notes: Aberdeen noted HP not making major investments in Convex HPTC technology; effectively sidelined.

metric_value: Uncertain long-term commitment to HPTC
notes: Sun recently entered HPTC via Floating Point Systems (Cray) and Thinking Machines acquisitions; Aberdeen questioned commitment.

metric_value: 5 primary criteria
notes: Aberdeen identified 5 HPTC evaluation criteria: ISV application availability, scaleable product line, cost-effectiveness, worldwide support, NT-Unix transition path.

metric_value: HPTC customers demand both Unix and NT options
notes: Customer criteria explicitly included NT-Unix dual platform support capability.

metric_name: HPTC application segments targeted by Digital
metric_value: EDA, MCAD, geoscience, energy, financial services, biosciences, entertainment
notes: Digital targeted 7 HPTC application segments including both traditional and emerging markets.

metric_name: Demand driver for high-performance Unix compute servers
metric_value: NT workstation expansion driving need for Unix compute backend
notes: NT personal workstation adoption expanding technical market and driving complementary Unix compute server demand.

metric_value: Standalone HPTC group + Visual Computing Workstation group
notes: Digital created standalone HPTC group and complementary Visual Computing Workstation group; separate P&L units.

metric_value: Four quadrant coverage: Unix/NT x workstation/server
notes: Digital mapped products across 4 High Performance Quadrants (Unix server, NT server, Unix workstation, NT workstation).

metric_value: Dual OS support on single Alpha hardware
notes: Digital's strategy allowed Unix and NT on single Alpha hardware; dynamic reconfiguration in field.

metric_value: Digital #1; SGI #2; IBM #3; HP/Convex and Sun secondary
notes: Aberdeen ranked 3 key HPTC suppliers: Digital, SGI, IBM. HP/Convex and Sun described as 'secondary.'

metric_value: Well-positioned for additional market share gains over next several years
notes: Aberdeen predicted Digital would gain HPTC market share with its HPTC group, AlphaServer suite, and compute-server business focus.

metric_value: Looking overly expensive and slow for typical production HPTC
notes: Aberdeen predicted IBM's MPP architecture would be disadvantaged versus Digital's SMP/cluster approach.

metric_name: SGI NT transition gap as sustained Digital advantage
metric_value: SGI's lack of NT commitment gives Digital sustained competitive edge
notes: Aberdeen predicted Digital's dual Unix/NT capability would sustain competitive differentiation against SGI.

metric_value: Increasing Alpha clockrates will win more industry-standard benchmarks
notes: Aberdeen predicted Digital would win increasing numbers of HPTC benchmarks as higher-clockrate Alphas arrived.

metric_value: Compaq acquired DEC for $9.6B June 1998
notes: Largest tech acquisition at the time; HPTC group dissolved. Alpha continued under Compaq/HP until 2007.

metric_value: Compaq announced Alpha phase-out; IP sold to Intel 2001
notes: Compaq phased out Alpha in favor of Intel Itanium. Last Alpha system sold by HP in April 2007.

metric_value: SGI filed Chapter 11 in 2006; Chapter 7 in 2009
notes: SGI went bankrupt 2006/2009. Aberdeen's criticism of SGI's NT transition proved accurate but the company's decline was broader. Assets acquired by Rackable Systems (renamed SGI); later by HPE 2016.

metric_value: Linux-based x86 clusters replaced proprietary RISC HPTC
notes: By early 2000s, Linux/x86 Beowulf clusters dramatically disrupted proprietary Unix HPTC market; Aberdeen did not foresee this disruption in 1997.

metric_value: IBM RS/6000 SP evolved; IBM Power architecture dominant in HPC
notes: IBM's RS/6000 SP technology evolved into ASCI White and IBM Blue Gene systems. IBM Power-based systems dominated TOP500 list multiple times. Aberdeen's criticism proved too harsh.

metric_value: HP did not invest in Convex HPTC; Convex effectively abandoned
notes: Aberdeen's prediction that HP was not investing in Convex HPTC proved accurate. HP focused on mainstream servers.
